title: The moon followed me home
summary: A child wonders why the moon seems to move with the car on the way home.

title: Grandma and the new phone
summary: A young woman gives her old phone to her grandmother, who is not happy about it.

title: I found a coin
summary: A boy finds a coin on the street and doesn't know what to do with it.

title: Two friends, one painting
summary: Two friends visit a museum. One loves a painting. The other doesn't understand it.

title: I plant a tree
summary: An old man plants a small tree he knows he will not see grow tall.

title: The cat that waits at the door
summary: Mrs Tho's cat sits at the door every day at three. He is not waiting for food. He is waiting for someone special.

title: My grandmother's hands
summary: A grandchild watches her grandmother's hands all day. The hands cook, sew, feed chickens, draw, and tell stories.

title: The man who counts birds
summary: An old man counts the birds in his park every morning. The numbers are getting smaller, and the children start to help him.

title: Mrs Mai, you are not old
summary: A girl asks her seventy-year-old neighbour, a tailor, why she still works. The tailor gives a short answer — and offers the girl a sewing lesson.

title: The bread I bake on Saturdays
summary: A twelve-year-old bakes bread every Saturday. She started two years ago, after a Sunday her grandfather wanted fresh bread.

title: The neighbour's window
summary: A child watches a window across the road every evening for one minute. The window goes dark for a week, and the child finds out why.

title: Two hours, zero fish
summary: An eight-year-old boy and his uncle have been fishing for two hours and caught nothing. The uncle says they have caught something else.

title: My first job at the rice shop
summary: At thirteen, the writer was given one job at a small rice shop: count the bags. The old owner had a quiet way of checking that the new boy could be trusted.

title: The man who fixes umbrellas
summary: Mr Phuong fixes umbrellas on a street corner. He reads when no one comes. One day, a woman brings him a special umbrella.

title: Past the river
summary: Yen has had three small bad things in one day. Bao takes her home a longer way — past the river — where they look and do not talk.

title: The boy who waters the plants
summary: Khoa is nine. Every morning before the other children come, he waters twenty plants in his school garden — alone, and on time.

title: Vu's first haircut
summary: A six-year-old boy has his first haircut. His mother is at the hospital with his new baby sister. The barber tells him how the cutting will work — and shares a small story of his own.

title: The shoes I wear on Sundays
summary: A twelve-year-old keeps her late grandmother's house shoes in a box. On Sundays, she takes them out and sits at the family table.

title: Sixty-five years ago
summary: Two old men play chess in the park. One of them remembers a small thing about the other from when they were ten — and the other learns who his friend has been all along.

title: The third drawer
summary: A grandfather kept three drawers in his desk. The third was always closed. After he died, his family opened it.

title: Two umbrellas
summary: A short conversation at a bus stop. Khoa gives his umbrella to a stranger. She wants to know why.

title: The boy who counts crows
summary: Every morning, a small boy counts the crows on his way to school. His brother asks why.

title: Tell me when to stop
summary: An old barber and his longest customer. The customer cannot see the mirror anymore. He still comes every month.

title: The chestnut sweeper
summary: An old street sweeper in Lisbon cleans the square every morning. He does not sweep one thing. A boy asks him why.

title: A letter to Anna
summary: A grandmother in Krakow writes a short December letter to her granddaughter who lives in a new city far away.

title: The phone box library
summary: An old red phone box in a Welsh village is now the smallest library in the country. People leave books — and sometimes letters.

title: Three euros and ten cents
summary: A Munich bus driver finally asks his oldest passenger why she always pays in coins, never with a card.

title: Names in the flour
summary: A baker near Florence writes a different name in the flour on every tray of bread. A new shop boy asks him why.

title: The silence between steps
summary: A young tourist learns tango from an old teacher in Buenos Aires. She asks why they stop in the middle of the music.

title: Sara's first coffee
summary: A Swedish visitor in Saigon sits on a very small chair on the street and drinks coffee for the first time. The cup is small. The street is loud.

title: Somebody must see the sea
summary: A Norwegian lighthouse is now automatic. A computer does the work. But Mr Henrik still climbs the stairs every morning.

title: The oldest tree
summary: A mathematics teacher walks with his daughter in the Singapore Botanical Gardens. She wants the answer. He says he does not know.

title: The bow to the train
summary: A stationmaster at a small Japanese train station bows to every train, even when there are no passengers. A young reporter wants to know why.

title: The window cleaner
summary: A man cleans the windows of a Vienna café every Tuesday morning. He says he does not watch — he notices.

title: Two in the morning
summary: A locksmith arrives at two in the morning to help a young man who has lost his key. The young man asks why he still works nights.

title: One mouse, not one snake
summary: A boy walks into a Copenhagen pet shop and asks for a small green snake. The owner asks four small questions before she sells anything.

title: Do I have to play it?
summary: An old woman has not touched her late husband's piano in fourteen months. She calls a tuner. She has one question.

title: Giovanna at six
summary: An old Italian bell-ringer pulls the rope three times a day. A man from Rome offers him an electric machine.

title: The last camera shop
summary: A small Amsterdam shop has repaired old cameras since 1962. The owner keeps one box for a customer who died.

title: The beard before August
summary: A barber has not seen his regular customer for two months. The customer has been growing a beard for his daughter's wedding.

title: Pierogi in Krakow
summary: An Argentine student knocks on a Polish grandmother's door to learn to make dumplings. The grandmother gives no recipe.

title: Two yellow ribbons
summary: On a night train from Paris to Berlin, two strangers discover their bags are nearly identical. They make one small mark to tell them apart.

title: Three in the morning
summary: A night nurse in Sofia waters her patient's flowers at three in the morning. He never knows.

title: One sentence every night
summary: An old man in Prague has written one sentence a day for nine years. He explains to his granddaughter what the books are really for.

title: Four metres, forty metres
summary: After a small-town baseball game, a father who shouted from the stands wants to argue with the umpire. The umpire turns the argument around.

title: One clock a year
summary: A Geneva clockmaker makes one clock a year, names each one after a grandchild, and sells none. A buyer from a watch company offers two hundred thousand francs.

title: The king is slow
summary: An English grandfather teaches his nine-year-old granddaughter her first chess lesson. She likes the pawns. He explains why the most important piece is not the strongest.

title: Ana plays at five
summary: A Portuguese girl plays the violin in her back garden at four every morning while her fishmonger father drinks his coffee. One February day he asks her to play at the harbour.

title: Postcard from the hospital
summary: A young patient writes to his mother from his hospital bed. He cannot do much, so he lists seven things he sees from his window.

title: Nineteen names every Saturday
summary: Every Saturday at six in the morning, an old man cleans nineteen bronze plaques on the walls of a Madrid plaza — and says each name out loud.

title: A letter to the next tenant
summary: A man leaves a letter for the unknown person who will rent his flat after he moves out.

title: The bird and the neighbours
summary: Two neighbours have a small argument about a noisy bird in the morning.

title: I stopped wearing a watch
summary: A blog post about why the writer no longer wears a watch — and what happened.

title: Should we name the office robot?
summary: Two coworkers in an office argue about whether to give a name to the cleaning robot.

title: The empty bench
summary: A diary entry about a park bench where an old man used to sit every day.

title: The shop that opens at four
summary: Mrs Lan opens her small bread shop at 4 a.m. — before the city wakes — for the people who go to work before anyone else.

title: A seat on the train to Hue
summary: A young engineer finds a seat on a long train. The retired teacher next to him guesses his job from his shoes.

title: How my mother named me
summary: Lan's mother could not pick a name for her third child. Then a neighbour brought a small shy orchid that flowered on the day Lan was born.

title: The library card my father kept
summary: After their father dies, two siblings find a 1973 library card in his drawer. He never read books — so why did he keep it for fifty years?

title: The rice in the wedding pocket
summary: A girl asks her aunt about a small wedding custom — rice in the bride's pocket — and learns the quiet rules that go with it.

title: Cua the dog walks alone
summary: Cua is a small dog who walks himself to the fish shop and home every morning — and once saved his owner's life by sitting at the door.

title: The hand-painted house numbers
summary: Mrs Hue paints house numbers by hand. They are never the same. She says that is the point — a stencil treats every door the same, but she does not.

title: Why is the bowl ninety now?
summary: A regular customer asks the pho chef why the bowl is suddenly twenty thousand more. The chef sits down and walks through the numbers.

title: My grandfather's notebook of weather
summary: For forty-one years, the writer's grandfather wrote three lines about the weather every morning — but his notebooks were not really about the weather.

title: A jar of salt, or a list of neighbours
summary: A daughter is leaving for university in three days. Her mother tells the story of the jar of salt her own mother gave her — and then offers something different.

title: The woman who waves at the train
summary: For thirty-one years Mrs Phuong has waved at the express train. She started for one person. Now she waves for everyone — and forty-two letters say she has been seen.

title: After class
summary: A young teacher in her first year is told her class is boring. She finds the janitor in an empty classroom, and he tells her what he has heard for twenty-eight years.

title: My father's quiet Sundays
summary: Every Sunday at noon, the writer's father calls home — and for the first ten minutes, he says almost nothing. It took the writer twenty years to understand why.

title: Fix it for now
summary: A woman brings her father's broken watch to an old clockmaker. He can fix it two ways. She has to choose.

title: The cat in the office
summary: A small accounting office adopted a stray cat. The way people behave in meetings changed.

title: The wrong coat
summary: Two passengers on the same train picked up the same brown coat. Only one of them was supposed to.

title: Sunday repairs
summary: Every Sunday, Mr Bao fixes small broken things from his neighbours' houses. He does not need any of them.

title: The neighbour's piano
summary: For two years, a young pianist and her tired neighbour heard each other through the wall. They finally meet.

title: The last typewriter shop
summary: A small typewriter repair shop in Berlin has been open for fifty-one years. The owner is closing it in December. A young woman arrives with a broken typewriter.

title: The rules of the elevator
summary: A new Australian tenant in a Paris building meets the caretaker and asks about the old elevator. He gets a complete set of rules — and one strange one at the end.

title: What I learned in Hue
summary: A Dutch architect comes to Hue for one week to study old wooden houses. She stays for one month after one sentence from an old carpenter.

title: Letter to a forgotten writer
summary: Marko writes an open letter to a Polish author who died more than eighty years ago. Four people in his city meet once a month to read the author out loud.

title: Twenty-two years
summary: Two boyhood friends meet in Madrid for the first time in twenty-two years. They catch up — losses, jobs, an old anger — and then they go for coffee.

title: Two faces of the bakery
summary: A Vienna night-baker works alone from one to six in the morning. The morning customers do not see the night customers — but Mr Eduard does.

title: The garden between two buildings
summary: Five years ago, a piece of empty land in Berlin had only broken bottles. Now thirty-six families grow food and flowers there.

title: The honest audience
summary: A retired first violinist plays on a street corner in Prague. A young music student finally asks him why.

title: One boat a year
summary: A boatbuilder on the Greek island of Symi makes exactly one wooden fishing boat each year. A man from a big company visits with an offer.

title: Why we still write postcards
summary: A short editorial about why some people still mail postcards in an age of one-second email — and what a postcard can say that an inbox cannot.

title: Three minutes for the world
summary: A Stockholm radio newsreader has chosen the last 'small story' of the morning for thirty-one years. He has a wooden box of 1,400 letters.

title: Pick three
summary: A long-serving pool manager gives a new lifeguard exactly three rules. She also tells him how to find the third person to watch.

title: A list of small wins
summary: An Athens maths teacher has kept a notebook of 'small wins' from her classroom for years. Most students do not know it exists.

title: The bear stays
summary: A mother and her ten-year-old son sort old toys to give away. He keeps one — and asks his mother a small question back.

title: Letter to a future neighbour
summary: A woman moves out of her flat after twenty-two years. She leaves a letter for the next person who will live there.

title: Your grandfather's hand
summary: An umbrella repair shop in Edinburgh charges more to repair an old umbrella than a new one would cost. The shopkeeper explains why.

title: Three times scared
summary: A retired pilot watches planes with her twelve-year-old grandson at Amsterdam Schiphol. He asks if she was ever scared.

title: Bus 615
summary: A night bus driver in Helsinki waits two extra minutes for one missing regular. Two weeks later, he finds out who saw.

title: Twenty-four envelopes
summary: A short essay about why a friend sent paper wedding invitations in 2024 — and what the slow act was really saying.

title: Hands do not lie
summary: A young photographer asks an old Spanish fisherman to photograph his hands. He has one rule for her.

title: Alone, not lonely
summary: A widower in Madrid cooks Sunday lunch for one every week. His neighbour invites him over. He says no — and explains why.

title: The fridge that does not ask
summary: A small white community fridge in Manchester fed about three hundred families in 2024. Its rule has been the same since 2019.

title: The name on the label
summary: A Kenyan grandmother visiting London tells a museum guard the story of one small chair behind glass. She does not want it back.

title: A boring view
summary: A Helsinki translator has worked at the same window for nineteen years. A young colleague asks how he is not bored of the view.

title: Two weeks for a name
summary: The writer's friend has had a new cat for two weeks but has not given her a name. A short essay on why waiting can be the right thing.

title: Twenty-eight regulars
summary: A French waiter starts to teach a new colleague the Friday-evening regulars: who eats what, who never says thank you, and whose bread is never put on the table.

title: Sad men should eat soup
summary: A London journalist spends ten days at a Croatian lighthouse to write about the keeper. He ends up writing about the cook.

title: Empty squares at midnight
summary: An essay about why public squares feel strangely sad late at night, even when nothing has gone wrong.

title: A letter to a teacher I never thanked
summary: A former student writes, late in life, to a primary school teacher whose quiet influence he only understood years later.

title: The books that don't leave the library
summary: A young reader asks a librarian why some books cannot be borrowed, and gets an unexpected answer.

title: The man who collected broken umbrellas
summary: A small portrait of a man who picks up broken umbrellas after storms and gives them new names.

title: Should museums return ancient objects?
summary: A short podcast transcript in which a museum curator and a historian disagree about returning artefacts to the countries they came from.

title: The street before the city wakes
summary: Most of us never meet the people who clean our streets at five in the morning. This essay asks what it means to know a city deeply and be invisible to it.

title: The tree at the front of the lane
summary: A new neighbour wants to cut down a tree that blocks his living room's light. The elderly woman next door has fed birds in it for thirty-one years. A small conversation about how disagreements end without a winner.

title: The drawer of saved letters
summary: Most of us keep a few old letters for decades but delete thousands of emails without thinking. The essay asks what we actually buy when we save everything.

title: The bridge the village refused to demolish
summary: A district wanted to remove a 'useless' wooden footbridge. The villagers explained, gently, what they used it for now.

title: Are your online friends real?
summary: A grandmother is sceptical of her grandson's eight-year online friendship with a girl in Mexico. He answers with three small questions of his own.

title: The lost art of waiting
summary: We can have almost anything in seconds, and yet we are not happier. An essay on what we actually lost when waiting became unnecessary.

title: An open letter from the village librarian
summary: When the district council writes that nobody reads books anymore, the librarian replies — politely, firmly, and with a list of who actually walks through the door.

title: Restore the ruin, or leave it?
summary: A tour guide and an archaeologist disagree about whether to rebuild a half-fallen temple — and find a third option that neither of them brought with them.

title: My grandfather and the broken kettle
summary: When the writer was nine, his grandfather opened a kettle on the kitchen table and changed what the boy understood about repair.

title: Twelve hours on the road
summary: A journalist asks a long-distance bus driver what he sees from the front seat that the passengers don't.

title: The table set for ten
summary: The writer's grandmother set the family table for ten every Sunday for forty years — including one place for a husband long dead. After she dies, the writer inherits the ritual, and learns what the extra plates were really for.

title: A small untidy zone
summary: A beekeeper asks a city planner to keep part of a new park slightly wild. He is honest about whose career will benefit — and whose won't.

title: The letter I never sent
summary: Nine years ago the writer drafted a letter of apology to a strict teacher. She has never sent it. She explains, carefully, why writing it was the apology — and why sending it would have undone it.

title: Pretty but not real
summary: A singer brings a friend's painful comment to her old piano teacher. The teacher diagnoses it carefully — not in the way the singer expects — and offers a specific way out.

title: The library that does not lend
summary: A small library in northern Vietnam will not let its books leave the building. It has more readers, not fewer.

title: Across the wall
summary: Two long-distance pen friends — one inside a prison, one outside — meet for the first time in the visiting room.

title: The map maker of the village
summary: A retired postman draws a careful, deliberately inaccurate map of his village. He explains why his 'errors' are not errors.

title: The understudy
summary: An understudy who has waited three years for a chance to go on, and the lead actress, talk on the night before the lead's final performance.

title: Why Porto keeps its old bridge
summary: An iron bridge from 1886 still carries cars, pedestrians, and a metro line across the Douro river in Porto. Why does the city refuse, every twenty years, to replace it?

title: Ten years early
summary: A primary-school teacher in Edinburgh is retiring after thirty-eight years. A former pupil, now a teacher herself, comes to tell her something — and finds out where the lesson she still uses really came from.

title: I came to the Train Street twice
summary: A foreign travel writer visits Hanoi's famous Train Street first at the tourist hour, then at dawn. The coffee seller tells her what the visitors get wrong.

title: The curtain that drones don't notice
summary: The Faroe Islands deliver post by bicycle on routes a van cannot use. A drone might replace the bicycle — except for one detail nobody can automate.

title: Deep time
summary: On an Icelandic field site, a young intern asks a geologist what it is like to spend a career thinking in millions of years. Her answer is not what he expects.

title: Seven years on the same path
summary: An essay in defence of the route you do not change — and the slow kind of noticing that only repetition makes possible.

title: The cobbler's son
summary: Marco Bellini has mended shoes in Bologna for forty-one years. His son is a doctor at the main hospital. People keep asking the wrong question.

title: The last Tuesday
summary: An Irish late-night radio show is being cancelled. The host phones — or rather, takes the phone call from — her most regular listener, on the second-to-last Tuesday.

title: Why we still mend things
summary: A short editorial that defends repair cafés — not for the usual environmental reasons, but for the kind of attention that mending teaches.

title: The map archive in Antwerp
summary: Eighty-six drawers of historical maps — many of them wrong — line the basement of a museum in Antwerp. The archivist explains what wrong maps actually are.

title: Thirty-one pages
summary: A young translator in Tbilisi is eight months into a project nobody asked her to do: a book her grandmother wrote in 1954. She does not yet know what the project actually is.

title: Regret is for what you cannot change
summary: A retired ambassador has tea with his former protégé, now a senior diplomat. He asks her one private question. He gives her one private answer.

title: Postcards to prison
summary: A small English charity has mailed postcards to prisoners on behalf of relatives who stopped writing since 2003. It has one strict rule: no love letters.

title: The eight o'clock route
summary: A Buenos Aires city bus driver has read books aloud to his passengers on the evening shift for eleven years. The bus company has never written about it.

title: Believe the hands
summary: A pediatric nurse retiring after 37 years gives her replacement two days of training. She tells him the things that are not in the manual.

title: The third table
summary: A bookbinder in Coimbra has kept his father's workbench empty since 2019. He explains why a workshop needs a table where nothing is being done.

title: Not having a hometown
summary: A diplomat's child, now thirty-eight, describes what it is actually like to not have a single place to call home.

title: Breathe with Ana
summary: Backstage, a retired conductor gives a twenty-four-year-old violinist two minutes of advice before her first concert in a major German orchestra.

title: Show the joints
summary: A German filmmaker spends four days with a Vietnamese stilt-house carpenter and learns that the film she came to make is not the right one.

title: Tool inheritance
summary: A small movement among northern European carpenters argues for passing working tools — often outside the family — from one generation to the next. The writer defends the practice in three points.

title: Forty thousand is a lot of money
summary: An auction-house specialist gives a man two options for selling a painting that has been in his family for four generations. He chooses the one that pays less.

title: Four hundred and eleven mistakes
summary: A Norwegian cartographer keeps a private list of small differences between the country's maps and the actual land. Some are corrected; some are not.

title: The Saturday paper
summary: The writer's seventy-three-year-old father buys a Saturday newspaper every week and refuses to switch online. The writer eventually defends his choice — for three reasons that are not nostalgic.

title: The witness is yours
summary: A retiring court interpreter gives his replacement three rules before her first case. He also tells her why he is leaving.

title: The prompter's box
summary: For thirty-one years, an opera prompter at the Teatro Real has whispered the next line to singers who forget. The audience has never seen her face.

title: Friday belongs to Antonio
summary: Nine years of Friday evenings at the same restaurant: a short defence of the practice, and a small redefinition of 'boring.'

title: The voice as an object
summary: A library in Reykjavik for blind listeners keeps 31,000 audiobooks recorded by volunteers — including readers who have been dead for fifteen years.

title: The dignity of unfinished work
summary: An essay arguing that some of the most powerful human creations are valuable precisely because they were never finished.

title: Editorial: against smart doorbells in shared buildings
summary: A newspaper editorial argues that smart, camera-equipped doorbells in apartment buildings change the social contract of the hallway.

title: Architect and sociologist on housing
summary: A radio conversation in which an architect and a sociologist disagree about what a 'good' home actually is.

title: Returning to a home that no longer exists
summary: A short memoir about going back to a childhood neighbourhood and finding everything — and nothing — the same.

title: Letter from an aging translator
summary: An older literary translator writes to a young colleague about the strange, invisible craft of translation.

title: The cost of fluency
summary: Becoming 'native-like' in a second language is sold as pure gain. The essay argues that something specific is lost on the way — and that fluent speakers no longer notice they have lost it.

title: What to do with a poet's library
summary: A famous poet has died. His executor wants to break up the library; an archivist argues that the library, as a set, is itself a second body of work. They negotiate a third option neither came with.

title: The mathematics of forgiveness
summary: A debt of money can be cancelled with a stroke of a pen. A debt of harm cannot — and yet we keep trying to do the same arithmetic on both. The essay asks what forgiveness actually does.

title: When the replica is the more honest thing
summary: Lascaux closed in 1963 to keep its paintings alive; visitors today see a replica. The essay argues that an honest copy can be more truthful than a 'repaired original' presented as if it had survived intact.

title: Two words for two things
summary: A poet who wrote that the tech industry has 'killed the word attention' meets the attention researcher who felt called out by his essay. They debate whether the dwell-time number and the older meaning are even the same thing.

title: The vanishing third place
summary: Between home and work, there used to be a third social layer — the café, the bench, the quiet regular at the same window. The essay names what its disappearance has cost us and what could still be built instead.

title: A teacher's reply
summary: A parent has written to ask that her son's B+ be raised to an A 'for his university application'. The literature teacher replies — politely, firmly, and with an offer that has nothing to do with this grade.

title: The case for remembering badly
summary: We imagine perfect memory as a gift. The few people who actually have it report something closer to a curse. The essay asks what ordinary, imperfect memory is quietly doing for us.

title: The honest renovation
summary: An architect plans to convert a 1930s villa into modern flats, keeping only the facade. A historian argues that the facade is the cheapest part — and proposes a different kind of preservation.

title: The afterlives of objects
summary: When we die, the things we owned do not. They keep existing — usually badly chosen for their second lives. The essay asks what would change if we picked their next custodians while we were still here.

title: The architecture of waiting rooms
summary: We credit individuals for patience and impatience. The essay argues that the room they are waiting in is doing much of the work — and that an honest society would notice it.

title: Three tours in one week
summary: A tour guide notices the same elderly tourist on three of her tours in one week. She asks him why. His answer changes what she will say on tomorrow's tour.

title: An open letter from a retired translator
summary: After forty-three years, a translator writes to her younger colleagues — not with a list, but with one observation about who the work is really for.

title: The mouth of the kitchen
summary: Forty minutes before lunch service, a head chef tells his sous-chef something he has hidden for eight months — and gives her a new role she did not ask for.

title: The economics of being slightly late
summary: Lateness is treated as a personal vice. The essay argues that the social costs we punish, and the ones we tolerate, do not match the real harms.

title: What the river knows
summary: A hydrologist and an elderly villager argue, politely and seriously, about which kind of knowledge of the river should count.

title: An open letter to my city council, about benches
summary: A resident writes a calm, specific complaint about a recent design decision: the addition of armrests to the middle of public benches.

title: Two halves of a marriage
summary: A couple, married thirty-four years, sit at the kitchen table and discuss whether the parts of themselves they kept secret were betrayals.

title: The brushstroke that wasn't there
summary: When a conservator repairs a damaged painting, how much should she add back? An essay on the quiet ethical fault-line at the centre of European restoration.

title: The no I said in 1955
summary: After twenty-two recorded sessions, a documentary filmmaker asks an ageing cabaret singer one final question — and gets an answer that quietly rewrites everything she has been told.

title: Twelve minutes for whom?
summary: A Dutch town refused a highway upgrade that would have saved trucks twelve minutes. The decisive moment was one sentence at a community meeting — not an argument so much as a refusal of a hidden assumption.

title: Concrete will not be green
summary: An essay arguing that the search for a clean substitute for concrete is, for most infrastructure, a distraction from the harder and more important work of making concrete progressively less polluting.

title: Twelve technical, two real
summary: An old composer reviews his apprentice's score and explains the difference between the kind of error he can fix and the kind only she can.

title: Three years of mornings
summary: A foreign resident of Saigon writes about what she gradually understood — and did not — about the slow morning coffee, and what it taught her to do when she returned home.

title: His name is Jerome
summary: An office worker writes about realising, embarrassingly late, how long his floor's night janitor had been working there — and what that tells us about dignity, pay, and the cheap things companies could do.

title: One in a hundred
summary: On her final week, a Copenhagen tax inspector tells a junior colleague what she has actually learned about taxpayers in thirty-nine years — and why most of his training is preparing him for the wrong cases.

title: The chain, not the conservator
summary: A Berlin paper conservator works on 146 letters from a Jewish family in Hamburg, written between 1933 and 1942. She is sixty-one. She will not finish — and that, she explains, is not the problem.

title: What 'access' actually means
summary: Public libraries sometimes refuse to loan ordinary books — not rare ones — and the reasoning quietly reveals what librarians know about the word 'access' that the public mostly does not.

title: The blank plaque
summary: A Belgian town has, for forty-seven years, left a war memorial unfinished because one of the eight names was a collaborator. In 2022 a new committee inherited the question.

title: Two to five
summary: A thirty-two-year-old violinist receives a diagnosis she expected. Her hand specialist gives her an honest time range — and one piece of advice she did not ask for.

title: The paradox of the attention economy
summary: An essay arguing that platforms which sell our attention also depend on giving us back enough attention to feel chosen.

title: De-extinction: ethics on the table
summary: A transcript in which a geneticist and an ecologist disagree over whether bringing back extinct species is responsible science.

title: Open letter: against the cult of biographies
summary: An open letter questioning our growing reverence for biographies of remarkable people — and what such books quietly mislead us about.

title: The last lighthouse keeper
summary: A long-form report on the closure of a manned lighthouse, and the slow argument over what is actually being preserved when we 'preserve' a building.

title: Editorial: when ruins are protected and slums are not
summary: An editorial that asks why we spend public money defending an empty Roman wall while letting a living, occupied neighbourhood collapse around it.

title: The economics of trust in small communities
summary: Village shops once extended unlimited credit to residents and refused even a coin to visitors. The essay asks what we have traded away, and what online reputation systems imitate without the original limits.

title: A translator's third stanza
summary: A translator brings a stuck poem to the poet who wrote it. He surprises her by telling her to break it — and by explaining, gently, what an honest translation actually is.

title: The grammar of grief
summary: Every culture has a 'grammar of grief' — rules that stage mourning publicly. The essay asks what is lost when modern societies thin those rules out, and whether something equivalent can be built without restoring the old ceremonies.

title: What 'belongs' means
summary: A descendant arrives at a Paris museum to ask for the return of an eighteenth-century object taken from his family temple. The curator does not refuse — but she asks him to look carefully at the verb in his own sentence.

title: The conservation paradox
summary: A protected thing changes by being protected. The essay argues that conservation cannot save originals — only design honest hybrids — and that pretending otherwise turns preservation into a small lie.

title: Walkability and walked-ness
summary: A town planner brings her doubts to a sociologist: the algorithm scores a quiet new district highly and a lively old district poorly. He gives her a sharper vocabulary — and a way to use both numbers honestly.

title: The ethics of biographical memory
summary: Memoirs and biographies decide which version of a person will outlast them — usually without anyone naming the power that is being exercised. The essay proposes specific conventions that would make the form more honest.

title: The conversation belongs to him
summary: A patient's daughter brings a difficult question to the palliative care doctor before he sees her father. He reframes what is actually being decided — and gives her a single sentence to use.

title: On the slow death of expertise
summary: Expertise has lost ground in modern conversation. The essay argues that the cure is not louder claims of authority but quieter claims of competence — drawn more carefully than they used to be.

title: Defensible is not the same as required
summary: A journalist brings a story to her retired editor. He gives her four questions, then sharpens them — and shows her that the test she is using is not the test she should be using.

title: The grammar of refusal
summary: The English 'no' is one sentence. Many languages refuse only across three or four turns. The essay argues these are not 'polite' and 'rude' versions of the same act — they are different machines, optimised for different societies, and the real work is in the ear, not the mouth.

title: The right not to know
summary: An oncologist tells a patient's daughter that her father has refused to be told his prognosis — and has asked the doctor to tell her instead. The daughter must decide what to carry, and how.

title: The slow letter
summary: An essay on what we lost when correspondence stopped requiring patience — and what writing by hand still teaches.

title: The cut
summary: A documentary filmmaker and one of her subjects argue, two years after the film's release, over a sequence the subject now wants removed.

title: The dignity of unfinished things
summary: An essay against the tyranny of completion: some projects are improved, not impoverished, by being left unresolved.

title: The translator's question
summary: A literary translator and the living author whose book she is translating disagree, carefully, about one sentence — and what it reveals about the whole.

title: Letter to a former student, twenty years on
summary: A retired teacher writes back to a student who once asked her a question she could not answer at the time.

title: The forensic accountant and the heir
summary: A forensic accountant has finished her audit of a recently-deceased uncle's estate. The heir wants to know whether the truth in the books should stay in the books.

title: What does 'authentic' restoration mean?
summary: Heritage architecture has at least four mutually incompatible definitions of 'authenticity', and most public arguments about cathedrals, town halls and historic streets quietly collapse different definitions together. An essay on why the disagreement matters.

title: Six weeks against a yoghurt pot
summary: A corporate lawyer privately consults an ethicist about a supplier substitution she has tried — twice — to escalate inside her company. The conversation does not deliver advice so much as a careful map of the choice she is going to have to make.

title: Hellberg's tapes
summary: A forgotten Swedish archivist recorded 14,000 hours of ordinary national soundscapes between 1935 and 1962. Rediscovered in 2019, the tapes raise an unexpectedly sharp question about what heritage actually includes.

title: Memory as an editorial act
summary: An essay arguing that 'private memory' is, on inspection, an editorial responsibility — that the vocabulary of accuracy we already apply in journalism and law has no everyday equivalent for the way families and nations remember.

title: Translation as a second birth
summary: A Hungarian poet and her English translator review an English manuscript of her selected poems. They agree on most pages. Where they disagree, the negotiation is not about language but about who the author of a published poem is, forty-six years later.

title: A million small lawsuits
summary: An essay arguing that the European 'right to be forgotten' is, in aggregate, producing a slowly edited public record that no one ever consented to — and that the answer is neither absolute deletion nor absolute preservation.

title: Four sheets and one stream
summary: A foreign writer spends eight days with a sixth-generation washi paper maker in Ehime Prefecture and comes away resisting the metaphysical register Western writing usually applies to Japanese craft.

title: Presence, not vocabulary
summary: A retiring pediatric oncologist gives a second-year fellow a brief, deeply technical tutorial on how to deliver hard news to a family — and why most of what he is teaching is not, in fact, words.

title: The slow violence of urban heat
summary: An essay arguing that the main way climate change is currently killing Europeans is not floods or wildfires but urban heat — a slow, statistically diffuse harm whose politics requires a different lens than the dramatic-event framing of most climate writing.

title: Eleven speakers
summary: On the smallest inhabited Faroese island, eleven people still speak a dialect that the larger islands no longer recognise. An essay on what can — and cannot — be done about a language in contraction, and what the dialect actually carries.

title: Witnesses, not oracles
summary: An essay arguing that historians, when invited to predict contemporary political outcomes, are being used as oracles when their training equips them only to be witnesses — and that the conflation has measurable costs.
